1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The very brief verse notes the LORD speaking concerning the test of the rods. The grammatical voices note the introduction. Henry and Gill note that "the LORD spake unto Moses, saying" — the LORD spoke to Moses, now to give the sign of the twelve rods, by which the priesthood of Aaron would be vindicated (ch. 17); the speaking (God's command, the new section); the occasion (after the rebellion of Korah and the murmuring of the people against the priesthood, now the sign that would settle whom God had chosen, the budding rod ending the strife); the figure (God himself settling the question of the priesthood by a sign, that the murmuring cease; the truth that God vindicates his own appointments, the choice made plain by the sign). That God speaks concerning the rods shows his care to end the strife and confirm his chosen priest.

On "the LORD spake unto Moses," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the word. Calvin and Keil note the LORD speaking to Moses (the sign of the rods) — the new section (the test of the priesthood).

On the speaking, the grammatical-historical note the command: the LORD spake unto Moses — the sign of the rods introduced; the providence (God settling the priesthood by a sign). The verse displays the LORD speaking concerning the test of the rods.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the introduction — "the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," the rods (ch. 17, Henry, Gill); "the LORD spake unto Moses," the word (Calvin, Keil); the command (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the speaking LORD. The verse marks the speaking: to Moses. The verse displays the rods: the sign. The verse displays the priesthood: to be confirmed. The verse displays the strife: to end. The speaking displays God settling the priesthood by a sign. The verse displays the LORD: speaking. The verse displays the sign: appointed. The verse displays the choice: confirmed. The LORD speaking concerning the test of the rods displays the divine command: the LORD spake unto Moses (ch. 17) — God giving the sign of the rods, that the murmuring against the priesthood cease and his chosen priest be confirmed. The verse displays the twelve rods of the tribes (vv.2-5).
2:
"Speak unto the children of Israel, and take of every one of them a rod according to the house of their fathers, of all their princes according to the house of their fathers twelve rods: write thou every man's name upon his rod." The very brief verse notes the twelve rods of the tribes, each named. The grammatical voices note the rods. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "take of every one of them a rod according to the house of their fathers... twelve rods: write thou every man's name upon his rod" — a rod was taken from each tribe, twelve rods, each prince's name written on his rod; the take... a rod according to the house of their fathers (a rod from each tribe); the twelve rods (one for each tribe); the write thou every man's name upon his rod (each rod named); the figure (the twelve rods, the tribes represented, each named, set before God for the test; the rods, dead dry sticks, that God would make to declare his choice; the figure of the contest brought before God, each name written, the matter left to God's sign; the dead rods awaiting the breath of God, that one might live and so declare the chosen).

On "take of every one of them a rod according to the house of their fathers... twelve rods," the witnesses note the rods. Gill notes the twelve rods, one per tribe (the tribes represented) — the rods (the twelve).

On "write thou every man's name upon his rod," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the naming: "write thou every man's name upon his rod" — each rod named (the matter set before God); the figure (the dead rods awaiting God's sign); the providence (the rods prepared, Aaron's name on Levi's rod to follow, vv.3). The verse displays the twelve rods of the tribes, each named.

5:

8:
"And it came to pass, that on the morrow Moses went into the tabernacle of witness; and, behold, the rod of Aaron for the house of Levi was budded, and brought forth buds, and bloomed blossoms, and yielded almonds." The witnesses note Aaron's rod that budded, blossomed, and bore almonds — the crux of life out of death and the priesthood of Christ. On "the rod of Aaron for the house of Levi was budded, and brought forth buds, and bloomed blossoms, and yielded almonds," interpreters expound the sign. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the budding rod — "the rod of Aaron... was budded, and brought forth buds, and bloomed blossoms, and yielded almonds": overnight, Aaron's dead, dry rod, alone of the twelve, put forth buds, blossoms, and ripe almonds together, the full token of life; the witnesses note the significance — the dead rod made to live, the divine choice of Aaron's priesthood declared by the miracle of life; the lesson — the budding rod a figure of the priesthood of Christ, raised from the dead (the dead rod brought to life and fruit a figure of the resurrection, life out of death, the priesthood confirmed not by nature but by the power of God; the almond, the first tree to wake and blossom in the spring, Jeremiah 1:11-12); the New Testament use — Aaron's rod that budded was laid up in the ark, Hebrews 9:4, a memorial of the chosen priesthood; the figure — Christ the true High Priest, chosen of God and raised from the dead, "made... after the power of an endless life," Hebrews 7:16; the truth that God's choice is sealed by life out of death, the buds, blossoms, and fruit all at once the full witness of the living priesthood; The figure of life and fruit out of a dead rod, the sign of God's chosen Priest.

On "the rod of Aaron for the house of Levi was budded, and brought forth buds," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and witnesses note the budding. Ellicott and Henry note Aaron's dead rod budding alone (the divine choice declared by life, Jeremiah 1:11) — the budding (the dead rod made to live).

On "and bloomed blossoms, and yielded almonds," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the full fruit. Wesley and Gill note the blossoms and ripe almonds together (the full token of life, the priesthood of Christ raised, Hebrews 7:16; 9:4) — the figure (the resurrection life, the living priesthood); the providence (the rod budded, the rods brought out to follow, vv.9). The verse displays Aaron's rod that budded, blossomed, and bore almonds.

The witnesses agree the substance: Aaron's dead rod alone budded, blossomed, and bore almonds, declaring God's choice (Calvin, Ellicott, Henry, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of life out of death and the priesthood of Christ: the budding rod — the dead, dry rod made to live and bear fruit, the divine choice of the priesthood sealed by the miracle of life, a figure of the priesthood of Christ raised from the dead (laid up in the ark as a memorial, Hebrews 9:4), the High Priest made after the power of an endless life (Hebrews 7:16; Jeremiah 1:11-12). The verse displays the life-out-of-death crux. Aaron's rod that budded, blossomed, and bore almonds displays the crux: the rod of Aaron... was budded, and brought forth buds, and bloomed blossoms, and yielded almonds (Hebrews 9:4; 7:16; Jeremiah 1:11-12) — the dead rod made to live and bear fruit, the divine choice sealed by life out of death, a figure of Christ the chosen High Priest raised from the dead, made after the power of an endless life. The verse displays the rods brought out and shown to the people (v.9).

12:
"And the children of Israel spake unto Moses, saying, Behold, we die, we perish, we all perish." The witnesses note the people's despairing cry at the nearness of the holy — the crux of the terror of access to God without a mediator. On "the children of Israel spake unto Moses, saying, Behold, we die, we perish, we all perish," interpreters expound the despair. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the cry — "Behold, we die, we perish, we all perish": after the judgment on Korah and the sign of the rod, the people cried out in terror at the danger of coming near the holy things and the holy God; the witnesses note the significance — the despairing fear of the people (overwhelmed by the judgments, terrified at the nearness of the holy God, feeling that any approach meant death); the lesson — the terror of the sinner before the holiness of God without a mediator (the people, conscious of their sin and the deadly holiness of God, despair of drawing near; the fear that "who can stand before this holy LORD God?" 1 Samuel 6:20; "if a beast touch the mountain, it shall be stoned... so terrible was the sight," Hebrews 12:20-21); the cry of the soul that feels its sin and the consuming holiness of God; the figure — the need of a mediator, the terror of access without one, answered in the very next chapter by the charge of the priests who would bear the iniquity of the sanctuary, ch.18; fulfilled in Christ our great High Priest through whom we "come boldly unto the throne of grace," Hebrews 4:16; 10:19-22; The figure of the dread of the holy and the need of the mediator.

On "the children of Israel spake unto Moses, saying, Behold, we die, we perish," the grammatical-historical Clarke and witnesses note the despair. Clarke and Gill note the people's despairing cry, terrified at the judgments and the holy (the dread of the holy, 1 Samuel 6:20) — the despair (the cry of terror).

On "we all perish," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the terror. Wesley and Gill note the fear that all would perish at the nearness of the holy (the dread without a mediator, Hebrews 12:20-21) — the figure (the need of the priest who makes access, Hebrews 4:16); the providence (the despair, the question of access to follow, vv.13). The verse displays the people's despairing cry at the nearness of the holy.

The witnesses agree the substance: the people cried out in despairing terror at the danger of the holy (Calvin, Clarke, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the terror of access without a mediator: the people's cry — the despairing fear of the sinner before the holiness of God, the dread that any approach means death (1 Samuel 6:20; Hebrews 12:20-21), the terror that answers to the need of a mediator, met in the priesthood that bears the iniquity of the sanctuary (ch.18) and fulfilled in Christ through whom we come boldly to the throne of grace (Hebrews 4:16; 10:19-22). The verse displays the terror-of-the-holy crux. The people's despairing cry at the nearness of the holy displays the crux: Behold, we die, we perish, we all perish (1 Samuel 6:20; Hebrews 12:20-21; Hebrews 4:16) — the terror of the sinner before the holiness of God without a mediator, the dread that answers to the need of the priest who makes a way of access, fulfilled in Christ our great High Priest. The verse displays the cry of those who come near the tabernacle (v.13).
13:
"Whosoever cometh any thing near unto the tabernacle of the LORD shall die: shall we be consumed with dying?" The very brief verse notes the despairing fear that all who draw near must die. The grammatical voices note the dread. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Whosoever cometh any thing near unto the tabernacle of the LORD shall die: shall we be consumed with dying?" — the people, in their terror, feared that any who came near the tabernacle must die, and cried, must we all be consumed? the Whosoever cometh any thing near unto the tabernacle of the LORD shall die (the dread of the deadly holiness); the shall we be consumed with dying? (the despairing question, the fear of utter destruction); the figure (the terror of the holy that makes the sinner fear all approach is death; the cry of despair at the deadly nearness of God, the sense that the holiness of God would consume them; the truth that without a way of access the holiness of God is a terror, the sinner fearing to be consumed; the figure that answers to the need of the priesthood and the mediator, the question that the next chapter would meet with the charge of the priests, ch.18; the dread that prepares the way for the comfort of the appointed access).

On "Whosoever cometh any thing near unto the tabernacle of the LORD shall die," the witnesses note the dread. Gill notes the fear that all who draw near must die (the deadly holiness) — the dread (the terror of the holy).

On "shall we be consumed with dying?" the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the despair: "shall we be consumed with dying?" — the despairing question (the fear of utter destruction); the figure (the terror that needs the mediator, ch.18); the providence (the despair, the charge of the priests to answer it, ch.18). The verse displays the despairing fear that all who draw near must die.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the dread — "Whosoever cometh any thing near unto the tabernacle of the LORD shall die: shall we be consumed with dying?" the dread (Keil, Gill); "Whosoever cometh any thing near unto the tabernacle of the LORD shall die," the dread (Gill); "shall we be consumed with dying?" the despair (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dread. The verse marks the approach: death. The verse displays the tabernacle: deadly to draw near. The verse displays the despair: consumed. The verse displays the mediator: needed. The dread displays the terror of the holy without access. The verse displays the holy: a terror. The verse displays the sinner: in despair. The verse displays the access: feared. The despairing fear that all who draw near must die displays the terror that needs the mediator: shall we be consumed with dying? (ch.18) — the dread of the deadly holiness, the cry of despair at the nearness of God, the terror that the next chapter would answer with the charge of the priests who bear the iniquity of the sanctuary. The verse closes the chapter, before the charge of the priests (ch.18).
